coredata.dc:title: A fluorescent probe for protein tyrosine kinase 7 detection in serum and cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Lujia
Ma, Yu
Zhang, Huaiyin
Tan, Yiping
Zhu, Ling
Liu, Yongjie
Yin, Jiacheng
Chen, Qianqian
Yang, Dongzhi
Qian, Yi
Ma, Yunsu
coredata.dc:description: Tyrosine protein kinase 7 (PTK7) is overexpressed in breast cancer, which is considered as a cancer marker for breast cancer diagnosis. Therefore, a simple fluorescent probe for PTK7 detection and cell imaging was developed. In the developed probe, Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles were used as the fluorescent separator, and the fluorescence of carbon dots were used as the detection signal. The probe was worked by control the configurations of the aptamer of PTK7, the aptamer would be open chains by recognition of PTK7, which bond with carbon dots and show fluorescent signal. Based on the remarkably high affinity and selectivity of aptamer for PTK7, the excellent fluorescence property of carbon dots and the outstanding magnetism of Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles, the developed probe showed satisfied results for PTK7 detection in serum and MCF-7 cell imaging. The probe detected PTK7 in the range of 0.2–200 ng mL−1 with a detection limit of 0.0347 ng mL−1, and successfully imaged the cancer cell expressed PTK7. The results indicate that the nano-fluorescent probe has great potential for clinical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence probe
PTK7
Serum
Cell imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of Fe3O4 MNPs. (B) TEM images of b-CDs, Insert: the solution of b-CDs under day light and UV-lamp, respectively and high-resolution TEM image. (C) Magnetization curves of Fe3O4 MNPs, Insert image: the solution before and after separation by magnets under day light.
(A) FT-IR spectra of b-CDs and Fe3O4 MNPs. (B) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of b-CDs.
(A) UV–vis absorption spectra and zeta potential (B) of Fe3O4 MNPs, sgc8 and Fe3O4-sgc8.
The AGE results (A) and zeta potentials (B) of b-CDs, cDNA and b-CDs-cDNA.

The optimum reactive conditions of PTK 7 determination. The temperature (A) and the reactive time (B) of Fe3O4-sgc8, PTK7 and b-CDs-cDNA hybridization reaction. All measurements were performed by single-factor test.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the probe in the presence of various concentrations of PTK7 in range of 0.2–200 ng mL−1. (B) Fitting curve of the probe responded to versus concentration of PTK7 in range of 0.2–200 ng mL−1 (C) Linear fitting curve of fluorescence intensity versus lgPTK7 in range of 0.2–20 ng mL−1).

The selectivity (A) and anti-interference ability (B) of the developed probe for PTK 7 detection. The concentration of PTK 7 was 100 ng mL−1, while the concentrations of interfering materials (BSA, MB, AβO, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Cu2+, K+, Glu, Gul, Arg and mixture of them) were also 1000 ng mL−1

Laser confocal microscopy observation of nano-fluorescent probe cells on MCF-7 cells, (A) MCF-7 cells incubated with CDs-cDNA alone, (B) MCF-7 cells co-incubated with Fe3O4-sgc8/b-CDs-cDNA. From left to right, bright-field, fluorescence, and combination of bright-field and fluorescence plots (The scale bar is 20 μm).
Preparation process and PTK 7 detection mechanism of the developed fluorescence probe.
Comparison of the probe performance of this Work with those previously reported sensing methods.

head.ce:title: A fluorescent probe for protein tyrosine kinase 7 detection in serum and cell imaging

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Pharmacy, Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou, Jiangsu 22004, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Lujia Shi and Yu Ma contributed to this work equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tyrosine protein kinase 7 (PTK7) is overexpressed in breast cancer, which is considered as a cancer marker for breast cancer diagnosis. Therefore, a simple fluorescent probe for PTK7 detection and cell imaging was developed. In the developed probe, Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles were used as the fluorescent separator, and the fluorescence of carbon dots were used as the detection signal. The probe was worked by control the configurations of the aptamer of PTK7, the aptamer would be open chains by recognition of PTK7, which bond with carbon dots and show fluorescent signal. Based on the remarkably high affinity and selectivity of aptamer for PTK7, the excellent fluorescence property of carbon dots and the outstanding magnetism of Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles, the developed probe showed satisfied results for PTK7 detection in serum and MCF-7 cell imaging. The probe detected PTK7 in the range of 0.2–200 ng mL−1 with a detection limit of 0.0347 ng mL−1, and successfully imaged the cancer cell expressed PTK7. The results indicate that the nano-fluorescent probe has great potential for clinical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the incidence and mortality rate of cancer are increasing year by year. Lung cancer, rectal cancer and breast cancer have extremely high incidence and mortality rates in both developed and developing countries [1]. The latest cancer reports showed that breast cancer has become the number one cancer for women in the world [2]. Early diagnose is an important part of treatment for cancer. The level of biomarkers in body fluids (such as blood, urine, saliva, sweat) correlates with disease progression. The detection of tumor markers in body fluids is less damaging to the patient that makes it a safe, effective and convenient method for early diagnosis of cancer [3,4]. Many methods have been used to detect tumor markers in peripheral blood and body fluids, such as electrochemical assays [5], enzyme-linked immunesorbent assays (ELISA) [6], and fluorometric assays [7]. The electrochemical assay is sensitive but unstable, ELISA is accurate but time-consuming, fluorescence assays offers simple operation, fast analysis, low cost and high sensitivity, making it an effective method for the detection of a wide range of tumor markers [8–10].

The reported nanomaterials for fluorescent detection of tumor markers include carbon dots (CDs), quantum dots, polydopamine microspheres, magnetic nanoparticles, etc [11–14]. With the development of nanomaterials, fluorescent nanomaterials has been extensive replaced traditional dye in bio-sensing. CDs have good aqueous solubility, photostability, low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility and are widely used as fluorescent nanomaterials for fluorescence analysis [15]. Generally speaking, quenching agents play an important role in fluorescence probe. In there, the quencher need be have widely absorption of fluorescence and stable physical and chemical properties [16]. Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles (Fe3O4 MNPs) are of great interest in the field of nanomaterials because of their simple preparation, unique magnetic properties that can be easily enriched and separated, and strong absorption properties that can be used as fluorescence quencher [17].

These fluorescent nanomaterials for tumor marker detection are mainly based on ligand-receptor interactions. Aptamers are single-stranded nucleic acid molecules DNA or RNA that can bind to their targets through the principle of base complementary pairing [18]. With the properties of specific recognition of targets, easy synthesis and modification as well as excellent biocompatibility, aptamers have become popular molecules for basic research, diagnosis and treatment of diseases, especially in the field of cancer research [19]. They can specifically bind a variety of substances (proteins, nucleic acids, ions, glucose, etc.) with DNA conformations changing after binding to produce specific changes [20–24]. The aptamer conformational changes can expose base sites that introduce a fluorescent signal by pairing with their complementary pairing, achieving quantitative fluorescence detection of the target [25]. Meanwhile, the conformation transitions changed the length of aptamer. The fluorescence can be show or not by control the distance between fluorescent nanomaterial and quencher [20]. So, nucleic acid aptamers have obvious advantages for DNA sensors construction.

Protein tyrosine kinase 7 (PTK7) is a pseudo-protein kinase that regulates WNT-related pathways and is highly expressed in breast cancer cells, and is a common tumor marker for breast cancer [26,27]. Therefore, we can use fluorometric analysis to detect PTK7 in body fluids for early cancer diagnosis. Due to the low concentration of PTK7 in body fluids, it is required that the constructed nano-fluorescent probes have high sensitivity. CDs have the advantage of high fluorescence intensity at lower concentrations due to their excellent fluorescence properties, and Fe3O4 MNPs are good at quenching and separation, and they can be used to construct nano-fluorescent probes for the detection of PTK7. Moreover, Sgc-8 is a well-characterized hairpin DNA aptamer (41 bases) that specifically recognizes PTK7 [28]. It can open their small hairpin structure by recognizes and connects PTK7, that change the length of itself [29]. Thus, sgc-8 can be used as part of a nano-fluorescent probe that specifically recognizes PTK7, making the probe highly selective.

Aptamer-based fluorescent nano-probes can be used not only for cancer detection in serum but also for cell imaging. Live cell imaging technique has become an indispensable part of modern cell biology [30]. Cell imaging, which visualizes the expression of PTK7 in breast cancer cells through fluorescent images, is a typically non-destructive monitoring technique, making it a very useful tool in the diagnosis of breast cancer [31]. The fluorescent probes enable quantitative analysis of PTK7 as well as fluorescence imaging for cancer cells, providing additional information on breast cancer [32]. Live cell imaging technology can also be used for drug tracking and cell biopsies, expanding the application of probes.

So, this project proposes to construct a fluorescent probe based on composite nanomaterials and aptamers for PTK7 detection and cell imaging. The high biocompatibility and easy magnetic separation of Fe3O4 can be used as a carrier for the aptamer, and the specific recognition of the target PTK7 by the aptamer can be used to improve the selectivity of the probe, and then the excellent fluorescence emission of CDs can be used as the detection signal of PTK7 by its fluorescence intensity. Methodological validation of the established probes was carried out to investigate their practical value. Ultimately, it will be applied to PTK7 serum detection and MCF-7 cells imaging assays.

To demonstrate the successful synthesis of b-CDs (blue carbon dots) and Fe3O4 MNPs, TEM characterizations were performed. As shown in Fig. 1 A, Fe3O4MNPs displayed spherical shape with the particle size of about 400 nm, which is consistent with the reference reported [33]. Meanwhile, Fig. 1B showed the TEM images of b-CDs, and the insets from left to right are pictures of b-CDs taken under sunlight and UV light, respectively. As shown, b-CDs solution was colorless and transparent under sunlight, and it had blue fluorescence emission under UV light. As shown in Fig. S1, b-CDs had the maximum fluorescence intensity under the excited wavelength of 350 nm. The prepared b-CDs showed excitation wavelength independent emission. Fig. 1B showed that the b-CDs are approximately spherical particles with a size of 3.0 nm and uniform distribution, the inset image in Fig. 1B shows the lattice spacing of 0.22 nm, which is consistent with the b-CDs in reference [34]. As shown in Fig. 1C, the magnetic hysteresis loops of the Fe3O4 MNPs exhibits super paramagnetic behavior, meaning it an efficient separator. The inset image showed that Fe3O4 MNPs had a good dispersibility in aqueous solution and quickly separated under the action of a magnet, indicated that Fe3O4 MNPs had good magnetic properties. These results indicated that b-CDs and Fe3O4 MNPs were successful prepared.

Complementarily, the FTIR spectra also confirmed the successful synthesis of b-CDs and Fe3O4 MNPs (Fig. 2 A). The absorption bands at approximately 3400 cm−1 of b-CDs and Fe3O4 MNPs were corresponded to –OH/N–H stretching vibration. The broad vibration band at 3000 cm−1 was ascribed to C–H stretching vibration. Meanwhile, a band at 1680 cm−1 and a broad peak at 1200 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibration of CO and C–O, respectively [10,35]. The FT-IR results indicated that the existence of –COOH on the surface of b-CDs and Fe3O4 MNPs. Furthermore, the absorption peak around 1580 cm−1 of b-CDs was ascribed to the vibration of CC in benzene ring, which indicated the presentence of aromatic structures in b-CDs. The characteristic peak in the spectrum of Fe3O4 MNPs was appeared near 700 cm−1, which was ascribed to the stretching vibrations of Fe–O [36].

Then, the UV–Vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of b-CDs were measured. As shown in Fig. 2B, b-CDs showed two absorptions peaks at ∼250 nm and ∼350 nm, which could be attributed to the π–π* transition of the CC aromatic ring structure and then–π* transition of the CO, respectively [34]. The results correspond to the FTIR spectra results described as above. As shown in Fig. 2B, the b-CDs had a maximum fluorescence emission at 450 nm under the excitation at 350 nm. Combined with their images in Fig. 1B, they indicated that the prepared b-CDs have excellent fluorescence properties and are suitable as reporter signals for the construction of fluorescent sensors.

In this study, a composite nanomaterial-based fluorescent probe was designed for PTK7 detection and cell imaging. Scheme 1 depicted the preparation of probes and the mechanism for PTK7 detecting. Firstly, the carboxylated Fe3O4MNPs were connected with amino-modified sgc8 (aptamer of PTK7) through amide bonds to form Fe3O4-sgc8. Meanwhile, b-CDs were ligated with cDNA (the complementary strand at the end of sgc8) by amide bonds to form b-CDs-cDNA, too. Then, the sensor was developed by Fe3O4-sgc8 and b-CDs-cDNA. As PTK7 presented in the sensing system, which would be specifically recognized and bonded by sgc8 in Fe3O4-sgc8. The opened clip structure of sgc8 exposed the new base site which complementary to b-CDs-cDNA, that can bind to it to form (Fe3O4-sgc8/PTK7)-(b-CDs-cDNA). In addition, the hairpin structure of Fe3O4-sgc8 would be not opened while the absence of PTK7, which made it cannot recognize b-CDs-cDNA. Then, the free b-CDs-cDNA was removed by magnetic separation, and the quantitative detection of PTK7 was achieved by fluorescence intensity measuring. As PTK7 was highly expressed on the surface of human breast cancer cells (MCF-7), the developed sensor also could be used for MCF-7 cell imaging.

To verify the successful connection between Fe3O4 MNPs and sgc8, the UV–Vis spectra of Fe3O4 MNPs, sgc8, and Fe3O4 MNPs-sgc8 were detected. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the spectrum of Fe3O4 MNPs showed a broad absorption band within 200–800 nm, which indicated that Fe3O4 MNPs has a broad-spectrum quenching ability [17]. The spectrum of sgc8 showed a significant absorption peak near 260 nm [37], which ascribed to the conjugated double bond of its bases. As shown, the spectrum of Fe3O4-sgc8 presented both a broad absorption band (200–800 nm) and a characteristic peak near 260 nm, demonstrating the successful linkage of Fe3O4 MNPs and sgc8 [10]. Additionally, the zeta potential was measured to further demonstrate the successful connection of Fe3O4 MNPs and sgc8. As shown in Fig. 3B, Fe3O4 MNPs have a negative potential value due to the carboxyl group on the surface while sgc8 has a smaller one as the amino group modified it. The Fe3O4MNPs zeta potential decreased after the aptamer coupling, indicating the successful preparation of Fe3O4-sgc8 [38]. Therefore, these results proved the successful connection of Fe3O4 MNPs and sgc8.

To verify the linkage between b-CDs and cDNA, agarose gel electrophoresis (AGE) experiments were performed. As shown in Fig. 4 A, after the application of electric field, there is no fluorescence of Gel-red presented while the fluorescence of b-CDs located behind the dot-like position, which caused by the electropositive b-CDs and absent of DNA. Fig. 4A showed that b-CDs-cDNA was moved slower than cDNA, which confirmed the connection of b-CDs and cDNA [35]. Meanwhile, the change of electrophoretic mobility might be caused by the bigger molecular weight and smaller charge of b-CDs-cDNA [39]. Then, the zeta potential of them was measured to prove this guesswork. As shown in Fig. 4B, b-CDs have a positive potential with smaller absolute value, which caused by the carboxyl and amino group on their surface neutralized the charge of them. Moreover, cDNA and b-CDs-cDNA showed negative potential, and the absolute value of b-CDs-cDNA was between b-CDs and cDNA. This phenomenon might be caused by the consumption of carboxyl groups of b-CDs and amino groups of cDNA through the formation of b-CDs-cDNA [40]. With the formation of amide bonds in b-CDs-cDNA, the charges on the surface of b-CDs-cDNA were neutralized [39,41]. These results verified the successful linkage of b-CDs to cDNA.

Under the same experimental conditions, the effect of different reaction temperatures on the response ability of the nano-fluorescent probe was investigated. After incubating 60 μL b-CDs-cDNA, 60 μL Fe3O4 MNPs and 120 μL PTK7 (100 ng mL−1) at different temperatures (4, 25, 37, 50 °C) for 30 min, the fluorescence intensity values were determined. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence intensity increased with the increase of temperature until 37 °C, and then it decreased. Therefore, 37 °C was chosen as the best reaction temperature for PTK7 detection. Next, the reaction time of sensing was optimized by variable-controlling approach. As shown in Fig. 5B, the fluorescence intensity increased with reaction time increasing until 30 min, and then it changed with an opposite trend. The decrease of fluorescence intensity might be caused by the adsorption or aggregation of sensor. Therefore, a reaction time of 30 min was selected as the optimal reaction time for PTK7 detection.

Under the optimal conditions, PTK7 was measured by the developed probe. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the fluorescence intensity at 450 nm gradually increased as the concentration of PTK7 increased. As shown in Fig. 6B, the fluorescence intensity was positively correlated with the concentration of PTK7 in the range of 0.2–200 ng mL−1. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity showed a good linear relationship with the log of PTK7 concentration in the range of 0.2–20 ng mL−1. The linear relationship was obtained (n = 3): I = 238.1735 (lg C) + 208.3691, r = 0.998, (Fig. 6C). Then, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 0.0347 ng mL−1 by LOD = 3σ/k, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank sample (n = 10) and k is the slope of the calibration curve. To evaluate the sensitivity of this nano-fluorescent probe for the PTK7 quantitative detection, we compared the LOD of the prepared probe for PTK7 with the existing methods. As shown by Table 1 , the LOD obtained in this work are more sensitive than they are or equivalently [42–44]. The results showed that the prepared sensor has good sensitivity for PTK7 detection.

The precision and accuracy of the developed method for PTK7 detection were investigated. The results were shown in Table S1. The inter-day and intra-day accuracies were in the range of 95.5–102.7%, and RSDs in the range of 1.6–8.7%, which all met the requirements of biological sample detection. The results indicated that the fluorescent probe has good stability and accuracy, which can be used for PTK7 determination in real samples.

As the composition of serum, it may interfere with the detection results of PTK7 in real samples. Therefore, the selectivity and anti-interference of the designed probes need to be verified. As shown in Fig. 7 A, the prepared probe had excellent responses for PTK7, while the responses of it to the interfering materials were similar to that to PBS. The results verified that the probe has a high selectivity for PTK7 detection. As shown in Fig. 7B, there was almost no difference in the fluorescence intensity between control group and the interference groups, indicating the addition of high concentration of ions in the reaction system had no effect on the detection of PTK7. The above experimental results verified that the designed probe has high selectivity and anti-interference for PTK7, which can be used for the sensing of real samples.

The practicality of the constructed nano-fluorescent probe was verified by measuring the PTK7 in mice serum. In order to eliminate the influence of other substances in the serum on the detection results, the standard addition method was used to determine the PTK7 concentration in mice serum. The results were showed in Fig. S2 and Table S2. The concentration of PTK7 in mice bearing breast cancer was 0.452 ng mL−1 while the healthy mice was not detected. The results showed that cancer bearing mice had a significant increase in serum PTK7, which verified the diagnostic value of PTK7 as a biomarker. The recoveries for PTK7 detection in mice serum was in the range of 102.0–106.1%, and the RSDs were from 0.2 to 5.3%. These results indicated that the developed fluorescent sensor had massive potential in the clinical detection of PTK7.

In this study, the nano-fluorescent probe system was used to observe the PTK7 which secreted on the surface of MCF-7 cell membrane to diagnose diseases. As shown in Fig. 8 , the blue fluorescence was not seen in the b-CDs-cDNA group, probably due to b-CDs-cDNA is electronegative and is blocked by the electronegative cell membrane, which does not easily enter the cell to image [45]. In contrast, the Fe3O4-sgc8/b-CDs-cDNA group showed blue fluorescence in the cell membrane, which might be caused by the probe specifically recognized PTK7 on the cell membrane and tightly bound to it. As the binding with cell membrane of (Fe3O4-sgc8/PTK7)-(CDs-cDNA) and the large particle size of them, the composite nanoparticles can enter into the cells through endocytosis for cell imaging diagnosis [46]. The results suggested that the nano-fluorescent probe specifically recognizes PTK7 on the surface of MCF-7 cells, specifically identify tumor cells and diagnose them by imaging, which showed the potential for clinical imaging diagnosis.

In summary, a fluorescent probe for PTK7 detection was established based on multiple nanomaterials (b-CDs and Fe3O4 MNPs) and aptamer. The fluorescence intensity of this sensor was changed by PTK7, which showed a good liner relationship with the log of PTK7 concentration. Then, this proposed probe was applied to PTK7 detection with LOD of 0.0347 ng mL−1, and the results of methodological validation showed that the prepared probe had high sensitivity, stability and selectivity, and could be applied to actual sample detection. The probe was also used to determine PTK7 in cancer bearing mice and healthy mice with successful results, which proved the diagnostic value of PTK7 as a biomarker. Moreover, the probe was successfully applied to MCF-7 cell imaging assay. The above results indicated that the developed fluorescent probe has a certain potential for application in clinical practice, which provided a new method for disease diagnosis.

Lujia Shi and Yu Ma performed the data analyses and wrote the manuscript. Huaiyin Zhang, Yiping Tan and Jiacheng Yin performed the experiment. Ling Zhu and Qianqian Chen contributed significantly to analysis and manuscript preparation. Yongjie Liu helped perform the analysis with constructive discussions. Yi Qian contributed significantly to the manuscript revision. Dongzhi Yang and Yunsu Ma contributed to the conception of the study. All authors reviewed the manuscript
All authors of this work consent to the publication of the paper.
All data analyzed during this study are included in this published article.

This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (61901405), General Project of Natural Science of Jiangsu Province, (BK20201156, BK20221215), Excellent Talents Scientific Research Project (D2019026), Postdoctoral Science Foundation of China (2020M671608), the Medical and Health Guidance Plan Project of Wuxi Science and Technology Development (NZ2019048), and Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training program for College students (201910313074Y).
Shi Lujia is a student at School of Pharmacy, Xuzhou Medical University, China. Her research interests include biosensors and biological applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multi-carbon dots and aptamer based signal amplification ratiometric fluorescence probe for protein tyrosine kinase 7 detection

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Physical principles of nanoparticle cellular endocytosis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zhang, H. Gao, G. Bao, Physical Principles of Nanoparticle Cellular Endocytosis, ACS Nano 9(9) (2015) 8655-8671.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ma Yu is a student at School of Pharmacy, Xuzhou Medical University, China. Her research interests include biosensors and biological applications.
Zhang Huaiyin and Tan Yiping are students at School of Pharmacy, Xuzhou Medical University, China. Their research focuses on synthesis of functional material.

Zhu Ling is a doctor at Department of Pharmacy, The Affiliated Jiangyin Clinical College of Xuzhou Medical University, Wuxi, Jiangsu 214,400, PR China. Her research interests include functional material applied to biosensors.
Liu Yongjie is a student at School of Pharmacy, Xuzhou Medical University, China. Her research focuses on synthesis of functional material.
Yin Jiacheng and Chen Qianqian are students at School of Pharmacy, Xuzhou Medical University, China.

Yang Dongzhi is a professor at School of Pharmacy, Xuzhou Medical University, China. Her interests is cancer imaging expert with a broad knowledge of molecular imaging, functionalized materials, and cancer theranostics.

Qian Yi is a pharmacist at Wuxi No.2 People's Hospital since 2019. She graduated from Nanjing Medical University with a master's degree in pharmacy. Her research interests include clinical pharmacology and pharmaceutical analysis.

Ma Yunsu is an associate professor at School of Pharmacy, Xuzhou Medical University, China. She was a postdoctoral researcher at Xuzhou Medical University, China since 2019. Her research interests include biological electronics and chemical sensors.
